questions.text: In mammals, which blood vessel would normally carry the largest amount of urea?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: In mammals, hepatic veins normally carry the largest amount of urea. The urea cycle, also known as the ornithine cycle, is a cycle of biochemical reactions that produces urea from ammonia. Ornithine cycle takes place in the liver. So, the vein leaving the liver called the hepatic vein possesses maximum urea.

The hepatic portal vein is a blood vessel that carries nutrient-rich blood from the digestive organs (intestines, stomach, pancreas, and spleen) to the liver. It allows the liver to process and modify nutrients absorbed from the digestive system before releasing them into the general circulation. This unique vascular arrangement ensures efficient nutrient regulation and metabolic functions in the liver.
Renal vein carries clean, filtered blood out of the kidney.

The dorsal aorta is the main artery that carries oxygenated blood from the heart to the rest of the body in vertebrates. In human anatomy, it is specifically the descending part of the aorta, which runs along the back (dorsal) side of the thoracic and abdominal regions.

questions.explanation: Hepatic vein carries largest amount of urea. Urea is produced in liver. Hepatic vein transports liver’s deoxygenated blood to heart for oxygenation. So correct option is A.

questions.text: The figure shows blood circulation in humans with labels A to D. Select the option which gives correct identification of label and functions of the part.
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: B-Capillary­-Thin without muscle layer and wall two cell layers thick
options.explanation: Nutrients, hormones, gases etc. can diffuse into tissue cells through capillaries and vice versa. It is thin walled, and only one layer thick resting on basement membrane.

options.text: C-Vein­-Thin walled and blood flows injerks/spurts
options.explanation: Brings blood from different body parts to the heart. It is thin­ walled and acts as low­ resistance conductor for blood flow.

options.text: D-Pulmonary vein-­Takes oxygenated blood to heart, pO2 = 95 mmHg.
options.explanation: Two pulmonary veins from each lung transport the oxygenated blood to the left atrium.

options.text: A-Artery­-Thick walled and blood flows evenly
options.explanation: Carries blood from heart to different body parts. It is thick walled and elastic. The flow of blood in it is intermittent.

questions.explanation: Two pulmonary veins from each lung transport the oxygenated blood to the left atrium.

questions.text: If due to some injury, the chordae tendinae of the tricuspid valve of the human heart is partially non-­functional. What will be the immediate effect?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: The flow of blood into the aorta will be slowed down
options.explanation: This option is not directly related to the tricuspid valve or the chordae tendinae. The aorta is a major artery that carries oxygenated blood from the left ventricle of the heart to the rest of the body. Issues with the tricuspid valve would not directly impact the flow of blood into the aorta.

The 'pacemaker' refers to the sinoatrial (SA) node, which is responsible for initiating the electrical signals that regulate the heart's rhythm. Damage to the chordae tendinae of the tricuspid valve would not directly affect the function of the SA node, which is located in the right atrium.

options.text: The blood will tend to flow back into the left atrium
options.explanation: This option is not accurate. The tricuspid valve is located between the right atrium and right ventricle, so damage to its chordae tendinae would affect the flow of blood between these two chambers, not into the left atrium.

options.text: The flow of blood into the pulmonary artery will be reduced
options.explanation: Correct. The tricuspid valve is located on the right side of the heart and prevents backflow of blood from the right ventricle into the right atrium. If the chordae tendinae are partially non-functional, the tricuspid valve may not close properly, leading to regurgitation of blood from the right ventricle into the right atrium. This can result in a reduction of blood flow into the pulmonary artery, which carries blood from the right ventricle to the lungs.

questions.explanation: The tricuspid valve is located on the right side of the heart and prevents backflow of blood from the right ventricle into the right atrium. If the chordae tendinae are partially non-functional, the tricuspid valve may not close properly, leading to regurgitation of blood from the right ventricle into the right atrium. This can result in a reduction of blood flow into the pulmonary artery, which carries blood from the right ventricle to the lungs.

questions.text: The most active phagocytic white blood cells are:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: No, eosinophils and lymphocytes are not the most active phagocytic white blood cells. Eosinophils are less abundant than neutrophils. Eosinophils are involved in allergic reactions and parasitic infections. Lymphocytes are not as active phagocytic white blood cells as neutrophils and eosinophils. However, lymphocytes are important for the immune system because they produce antibodies, which help the body to fight off infection.

Neutrophils and monocytes are the most active phagocytic white blood cells in the human body. Phagocytosis is the process of engulfing and destroying foreign particles, such as bacteria and viruses. Neutrophils and monocytes are both professional phagocytes, meaning that they are specialized in this process. Neutrophils are the most abundant type of white blood cell in the blood. They are the first line of defense against infection, and they are quickly recruited to the site of infection. Neutrophils can engulf and destroy bacteria within minutes of entering the bloodstream.Monocytes are larger than neutrophils and have a longer lifespan. They circulate in the blood for a few days before migrating to tissues, where they become macrophages. Macrophages are long-lived cells that are able to phagocytose large amounts of material. They are important for clearing up debris and dead cells after an infection.

Neutrophils are the most abundant type of white blood cell in the blood. They are the first line of defense against infection, and they are quickly recruited to the site of infection. Neutrophils can engulf and destroy bacteria within minutes of entering the bloodstream. Eosinophils are another type of phagocyte. They are not as abundant as neutrophils, but they are more specialized for fighting parasites. Eosinophils are also involved in allergic reactions.

Lymphocytes are not the most active phagocytic white blood cells. Macrophages are the most active phagocytic white blood cells. Lymphocytes are a type of white blood cell that is responsible for the body's adaptive immune response. They are produced in the bone marrow and lymph nodes. Lymphocytes can be divided into two main types: B cells and T cells. B cells produce antibodies, which are proteins that bind to and neutralize foreign invaders. T cells help to destroy infected cells and cells that are not recognized by the immune system. Macrophages: Macrophages are a type of white blood cell that is responsible for the body's innate immune response. They are produced in the bone marrow and are found in tissues throughout the body. Macrophages are phagocytic, meaning that they can engulf and destroy foreign invaders.

questions.explanation: Neutrophils and monocytes are the most active phagocytic white blood cells in the human body. Phagocytosis is the process of engulfing and destroying foreign particles, such as bacteria and viruses. Neutrophils and monocytes are both professional phagocytes, meaning that they are specialized in this process. Neutrophils are the most abundant type of white blood cell in the blood. They are the first line of defense against infection, and they are quickly recruited to the site of infection. Neutrophils can engulf and destroy bacteria within minutes of entering the bloodstream.Monocytes are larger than neutrophils and have a longer lifespan. They circulate in the blood for a few days before migrating to tissues, where they become macrophages. Macrophages are long-lived cells that are able to phagocytose large amounts of material. They are important for clearing up debris and dead cells after an infection.

questions.text: Which is the principle cation in the plasma of the blood?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: It is not principle because it occurs in small amount in plasma.
It is not principle because it occurs in small amount in plasma.
It is not principle because it occurs in small amount in plasma.

Blood consists of a watery fluid called plasma. Plasma is a faint yellow, slightly alkaline, viscous fluid. It consists of 90% water, 1% inorganic salts, 7% or 8% proteins and 1% of other substances. The inorganic salts in plasma occur as ions. Sodium is the main cation of plasma and chloride, the main anion. Calcium, potassium and magnesium occur in small amounts.

questions.explanation: Blood consists of a watery fluid called plasma. Plasma is a faint yellow, slightly alkaline, viscous fluid. It consists of 90% water, 1% inorganic salts, 7% or 8% proteins and 1% of other substances. The inorganic salts in plasma occur as ions. Sodium is the main cation of plasma and chloride, the main anion. Calcium, potassium and magnesium occur in small amounts.

questions.text: The heart sound ‘lub’ is produced when:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is the correct option. The "lub" sound occurs when the mitral valve and tricuspid valve, both located between the atria and ventricles, close to prevent the backflow of blood from the ventricles to the atria.

options.text: The semi­lunar valves at the base of the aorta get closed
options.explanation: The closure of the semilunar valves prevents the backflow of blood from the aorta and pulmonary artery into the ventricles.
The tricuspid valve is responsible for allowing the flow of blood from the right atrium to the right ventricle during diastole.
The mitral valve opens during diastole to allow the flow of blood from the left atrium to the left ventricle.

questions.explanation: Therefore, the correct answer is B) The semilunar valves at the base of the aorta get closed, as their closure contributes to the 'dub' sound of the heart.

questions.text: The wall of blood capillary is formed of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Hemocytes are cells found in invertebrates, particularly in the hemolymph, which is the fluid equivalent to blood. However, in the context of blood capillaries, hemocytes are not involved in forming the wall of the capillary.
Parietal cells are a type of cell found in the stomach lining that secrete hydrochloric acid and intrinsic factors. They are not directly involved in forming the walls of blood capillaries.

Endothelial cells form the inner lining of blood vessels, including the walls of blood capillaries. They are thin, flat cells that create a smooth surface for blood flow and participate in various functions such as controlling the exchange of substances between blood and tissues.

Oxyntic cells, also known as parietal cells, are the cells found in the gastric glands of the stomach. They are responsible for secreting hydrochloric acid and intrinsic factors, aiding in digestion. Oxyntic cells are not involved in forming the wall of blood capillaries.

questions.explanation: Therefore, the correct answer to the question "The wall of blood capillary is formed of" is C) Endothelial cells.

questions.text: Lymph differs from blood in having:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Lymph contains white blood cells as well as blood. Hence, this option is incorrect.
Although lymph contains less they do contain proteins. Hence, this option is incorrect.
Lymph does not contain any red blood cells.
Lymphs do contain white blood cells and some proteins. Hence, this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: The lymph is a whitish fluid which is derived from the interstitial fluid that circulates throughout the lymphatic system. It contains a variety of substance like proteins, glucose, water, salts, fats and white blood cells. It does not have RBCs.

questions.text: Many _ posses small, thick leaves to limit water loss by reducing surface area proportional to the volume:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Hydrophytes are the plants which are completely or partially submerged in water. They cannot limit water loss because they live in water and they have large number of stomata which help in transpiration.

Yes, because xerophytes live in dry environments. They posess thick waxy cuticle over their epidermis to reduce water loss from internal tissues. They have less number of stomata to reduce the rate of transpiration. They store water with the help of special parenchyma cells, that's why they are called "succulent organs".

Mesophytes are plant species with a moderate affinity toward water supply. Most of the known plant species belong to mesophytes that require suitable growth conditions. Their root system and leaves are well-developed. They possess big leaves covered with a cuticle layer; they also have high growth rates. So, they cannot prevent transpiration.

They live in sea water and are adapted to salty environments. Salts enter in the bodies of such plants due to higher concentration in sea water. Hence, they also cannot prevent transpiration or limit water loss.

questions.explanation: Xerophytes live in dry environments. They posess thick waxy cuticle over their epidermis to reduce water loss from internal tissues. They have less number of stomata to reduce the rate of transpiration. They store water with the help of special parenchyma cells, that's why they are called "succulent organs".

questions.text: Lymph capillaries join to form:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: No, lymph capillaries join to form larger and larger lymph vessels and ultimately form thoracic lymphatic duct which opens into sub-clavian vein. The flow of lymph is always towards the thoracic duct.
Lymph capillaries join to form larger and larger lymph vessels and ultimately form thoracic lymphatic duct which opens into sub-clavian vein. The flow of lymph is always towards the thoracic duct.

No, lymph capillaries join to form larger and larger lymph vessels and ultimately form thoracic lymphatic duct which opens into sub-clavian vein. The flow of lymph is always towards the thoracic duct.

No, lymph capillaries join to form larger and larger lymph vessels and ultimately form thoracic lymphatic duct which opens into sub-clavian vein. The flow of lymph is always towards the thoracic duct.

questions.explanation: Lymph capillaries join to form larger and larger lymph vessels and ultimately form thoracic lymphatic duct which opens into sub-clavian vein. The flow of lymph is always towards the thoracic duct.

questions.text: The net difference between the fluid taken back by blood capillaries from interstitial spaces and the fluid given out by blood capillaries into the interstitial spaces in an average person per day is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: It is 3000 ml, not 1000 ml.
It is 3000 ml, not 2000 ml.

In an average person, about three liters more fluid leaves the blood capillaries than is reabsorbed by them each day. Lymphatic system returns this excess fluid and its dissolved proteins and other substances to the blood.
It is 3000 ml, not 4000 ml.

questions.explanation: In an average person, about three liters more fluid leaves the blood capillaries than is reabsorbed by them each day. Lymphatic system returns this excess fluid and its dissolved proteins and other substances to the blood.

questions.text: The heart is enclosed in a double membranous sac called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Heart is located in thoracic cavity, but is enclosed in pericardium.
Heart is located in chest cavity, but is enclosed in pericardium.

The heart of human is located in the chest cavity. The heart is enclosed in a double membranous sac the pericardial cavity, which contains pericardial fluid. Pericardium prevents the heart, prevents it from overextension and pericardial fluid protects it from abrasion.
Pleural cavity is present in lungs.

questions.explanation: The heart of human is located in the chest cavity. The heart is enclosed in a double membranous sac the pericardial cavity, which contains pericardial fluid. Pericardium prevents the heart, prevents it from overextension and pericardial fluid protects it from abrasion.

questions.text: The left atrioventricular valve is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is incorrect as the right atrioventicular valve is a tricuspid valve and not the left one.
This is correct as the left atrioventicular valve present between the left atrium and ventricle is bicuspid, composed of two cusps. Bicuspid valve is also called mitral valve.
Semilunar valves are present at the base of aorta and pulmonary artery in the heart.

Sphincters are the muscles that contracts or relaxes to open or close the passages in the body. Valves are the membranes for allowing the flow of fluid in one direction. Sphincters are mostly present in the GI tract.

questions.explanation: The left atrioventicular valve present between the left atrium and ventricle is bicuspid, composed of two cusps. Bicuspid valve is also called mitral valve.

questions.text: Chordae tendinae are attached to the:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Incorrect, as the chordae tendinae are attached to the papillary muscles as well as the cusps of the atrioventricular (AV) / Tricupid valves in the heart.

options.text: Papillary muscles and wall of tricuspid valve
options.explanation: Chordae tendinae are attached to the cusps of the atrioventricular (AV) / Tricuspid valves in the heart and the papillary muscles.
Incorrect, as the chordae tendinae are attached to the papillary muscles as well as the cusps of the atrioventricular (AV) / Tricupid valves in the heart.
Incorrect, as the chordae tendinae are attached to the papillary muscles as well as the cusps of the atrioventricular (AV) / Tricupid valves in the heart.

questions.explanation: The flaps of bicuspid and tricuspid valves are similarly attached through chordae tendinae to papillary muscles of the walls of ventricles.

questions.text: Pulmonary trunk is sub divided into:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Incorrect, as the pulmonary trunk is subdivided into two branches known as the left and right pulmonary arteries.
Incorrect, as the pulmonary trunk is subdivided into two branches known as the left and right pulmonary arteries.

The pulmonary trunk is subdivided into two branches known as the left and right pulmonary arteries. The pulmonary trunk is a large blood vessel that originates from the right ventricle of the heart. It carries deoxygenated blood away from the heart and towards the lungs for oxygenation. After leaving the right ventricle, the pulmonary trunk quickly divides into the left and right pulmonary arteries.
Incorrect, as the pulmonary trunk is subdivided into two branches known as the left and right pulmonary arteries.

questions.explanation: The pulmonary trunk is subdivided into two branches known as the left and right pulmonary arteries. The pulmonary trunk is a large blood vessel that originates from the right ventricle of the heart. It carries deoxygenated blood away from the heart and towards the lungs for oxygenation. After leaving the right ventricle, the pulmonary trunk quickly divides into the left and right pulmonary arteries.

questions.text: Coronary arteries supply the blood to the:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Coronary arteries supply blood to the heart, so this option is incorrect.
Coronary arteries supply blood to the heart, so this option is incorrect.

Coronary arteries supply blood to the heart muscle itself. The heart, like any other organ, requires a constant supply of oxygen and nutrients to function properly. The coronary arteries are responsible for delivering oxygen-rich blood to the heart muscle (myocardium), ensuring its continuous nourishment and providing the necessary resources for the heart to pump blood throughout the rest of the body. There are two main coronary arteries: the right coronary artery and the left coronary artery. These arteries branch out into smaller vessels, covering the heart's surface and penetrating into its muscle tissue, supplying blood to every part of the heart to keep it healthy and functioning optimally.
Coronary arteries supply blood to the heart, so this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: Coronary arteries supply blood to the heart muscle itself. The heart, like any other organ, requires a constant supply of oxygen and nutrients to function properly. The coronary arteries are responsible for delivering oxygen-rich blood to the heart muscle (myocardium), ensuring its continuous nourishment and providing the necessary resources for the heart to pump blood throughout the rest of the body. There are two main coronary arteries: the right coronary artery and the left coronary artery. These arteries branch out into smaller vessels, covering the heart's surface and penetrating into its muscle tissue, supplying blood to every part of the heart to keep it healthy and functioning optimally.

questions.text: The blood from the upper part of the body is collected by different veins, which join to form:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The aorta is the largest artery in the human body. It is responsible for carrying oxygenated blood from the heart to the rest of the body.
The pulmonary trunk is a large artery that carries deoxygenated blood from the heart to the lungs only.
Superior vena cava is the largest vein in the body that collects deoxygenated blood from the upper part of the body like head, neck, chest and arms to the heart.
Inferior vena cava collects deoxygenated blood from the lower part of the body.

questions.explanation: Superior vena cava is the largest vein in the body that collects deoxygenated blood from the upper part of the body like head, neck, chest and arms to the heart.

questions.text: 'Dubb' sound is produced by the closure of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The bicuspid valve is located between the left atrium and left ventricle, and the tricuspid valve is located between the right atrium and right ventricle. When these valves close, it creates a sharp, high-pitched sound called the "lub" sound.

The bicuspid valve is located between the left atrium and left ventricle, and the tricuspid valve is located between the right atrium and right ventricle. When these valves close, it creates a sharp, high-pitched sound called the "lub" sound.

The bicuspid valve is located between the left atrium and left ventricle, and the tricuspid valve is located between the right atrium and right ventricle. When these valves close, it creates a sharp, high-pitched sound called the "lub" sound.

The correct answer is Semilunar valves. The "dubb" sound is produced by the closure of the aortic and pulmonary valves, which are the semilunar valves. The bicuspid and tricuspid valves are atrioventricular valves, and they produce the "lub" sound.

questions.explanation: The "dubb" sound is produced by the closure of the aortic and pulmonary valves, which are the semilunar valves. The bicuspid and tricuspid valves are atrioventricular valves, and they produce the "lub" sound.

questions.text: What occurs, just prior to ventricular contraction:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: P wave represents excitation and occurs just prior to the atrial contraction.
T wave occurs just prior to the ventricles relaxation.

A normal electrocardiogram (ECG) indicates that the heart is functioning properly. The P wave occurs just before atrial contraction; the QRS wave occurs just before ventricular contraction and the T wave occurs when the ventricles are recovering from contraction.
S wave is a part of QRS complex which signifies final depolarization of ventricles.

questions.explanation: A normal electrocardiogram (ECG) indicates that the heart is functioning properly. The P wave occurs just before atrial contraction; the QRS wave occurs just before ventricular contraction and the T wave occurs when the ventricles are recovering from contraction.
